title: Exploring Data (EDA)
concept: Exploratory Data Analysis means getting to know your data first — sizes, averages, ranges, oddities — before drawing any conclusions.

options.text: Bad input data leads to bad results

exercises.explain: Right — quality of input decides quality of output.

cards.title: Meet your data first
cards.body: Before any fancy analysis, just look: How many rows? What's typical? Anything weird?

cards.title: What is EDA?
cards.body: Exploratory Data Analysis — summarizing and poking at data to understand its shape, spot patterns, and catch surprises before you conclude anything.

cards.title: Questions EDA asks
cards.body: "How many records? What's the average age? Any impossible values? Which category shows up most often?"

cards.title: Summaries & quick charts
cards.body: Counts, min/max, averages, and simple charts (a histogram or bar chart) reveal the data's story fast.

cards.title: EDA catches problems early
cards.body: It surfaces errors (age = 999), gaps, and surprises before they wreck your conclusions or your model. Skipping it is risky.

options.text: Getting to know your data — summaries, ranges, oddities

exercises.explain: EDA is the 'get to know your data' step before deeper analysis.

options.text: Checking the average and range of a column

exercises.explain: Summarizing columns (average, min, max, counts) is core EDA.

exercises.prompt: True or false: EDA can catch errors and surprises before you draw conclusions.
exercises.explain: True — that early-warning value is exactly why EDA matters.

exercises.sentence: EDA is short for ___ Data Analysis.
exercises.explain: Exploratory Data Analysis — exploring before concluding.

exercises.prompt: You're handed a brand-new dataset to analyze. Smart first step?

options.text: Explore it — check sizes, averages, and any weird values
Delete half of it to save space
Train a model on it blindly

exercises.explain: Always explore first. EDA tells you what you're working with — and what to fix — before you trust any result.
